header.subtitle: Module 8: The Integrated System — Tools that Connect
sec.visao.intro: In previous sessions, we studied each tool in isolation: Hooks, Memory, Agents, Skills, Commands, Plans, and MCP. Now let's see what happens when they work together. It's not a "golden triangle" — it's a complete system where each piece amplifies the others.
sec.visao.isolated.title: Isolated Tools
sec.visao.isolated.li.1: Hook runs a linter and shows the result
sec.visao.isolated.li.3: Agent executes a task and terminates
sec.visao.isolated.li.5: MCP connects to a data source
sec.visao.integrated.title: Integrated Tools
sec.visao.integrated.li.1: Hook detects error → triggers agent → agent fixes → hook validates again
sec.visao.integrated.li.2: Skill coordinates 3 agents in parallel with different roles
sec.visao.integrated.li.3: Agent queries MCP, generates a Plan, and saves decisions to Memory
sec.visao.integrated.li.4: Memory informs the agent about project patterns before each task
sec.visao.integrated.li.5: Command triggers a full pipeline: plan → code → review → commit
sec.visao.insight.title: The Composition Principle
sec.visao.insight.desc: Every Claude Code tool was designed as a composable primitive. Hooks are event listeners. Skills are packaged prompts. Agents are isolated processes. Memory is persistent state. MCP is external I/O. When you connect these primitives, the power isn't additive — it's multiplicative.
sec.mapa.intro: Before integrating, you need to understand each piece's role in the system. Every tool has a specific domain and clear interfaces with the others.
sec.mapa.matrix.title: Who connects to whom
sec.mapa.conn.hooks.title: Hooks →
sec.mapa.conn.hooks.desc: Can trigger scripts that spawn agents, update memory, query MCP servers, or run external validations. They are the system's "event bus."
sec.mapa.conn.agents.title: Agents →
sec.mapa.conn.agents.desc: Can call skills, create plans, read/write memory, use MCP tools, and spawn sub-agents. They are the "workers" that execute any combination of tools.
sec.mapa.conn.skills.title: Skills →
sec.mapa.conn.skills.desc: Can be invoked by agents, by commands, or even by other skills. They define the reusable "how to." They can coordinate multi-agents internally.
sec.mapa.conn.memory.title: Memory →
sec.mapa.conn.memory.desc: Automatically loaded at the start of every conversation. Agents and hooks can update memories. It is the "long-term state" that survives across sessions.
sec.mapa.mental.title: Mental Model: The Flow
sec.mapa.mental.desc: CLAUDE.md defines the rules. Memory brings historical context. A Command or Skill initiates the action. Agents execute the work. Hooks validate each step. Plans document the strategy. MCP connects to the outside world. All in a self-reinforcing chain.
sec.cenarios.intro: Each scenario shows different tools working together. Click the tabs to explore the combinations.
sec.cenarios.s1.label: Scenario 1
sec.cenarios.s1.title: Hook detects pattern → Agent fixes → Memory records
sec.cenarios.s1.desc: A PostToolUse hook detects that a TypeScript file was written without type exports. A script triggers Claude, which reads the error and fixes it. Memory records the decision so Claude already knows the pattern in the next session.
sec.cenarios.s2.label: Scenario 2
sec.cenarios.s2.title: Review skill coordinates 3 specialized agents
sec.cenarios.s2.desc: A slash command /review-pr invokes a skill that internally spawns 3 sub-agents in parallel: one for security, one for performance, one for tests. The skill consolidates results into a unified report.
sec.cenarios.s3.label: Scenario 3
sec.cenarios.s3.title: Agent Explore → Generates Plan → Agent Executor implements
sec.cenarios.s3.desc: A Plan-type agent analyzes the codebase, identifies relevant files, and creates a detailed plan. A second agent, with a clean context, receives the plan and implements it. The classic "Plan Then Build" but now orchestrated with typed agents.
sec.cenarios.s4.label: Scenario 4
sec.cenarios.s4.title: MCP queries Linear → Agent decides → Memory records
sec.cenarios.s4.desc: An agent uses a Linear MCP server to fetch active sprint tickets. Based on priorities, it generates a work plan and saves decisions to memory so the next session picks up where it left off.
sec.cenarios.s5.label: Scenario 5
sec.cenarios.s5.title: Pipeline: Command → Plan → Code → Review → Memory → Commit
sec.cenarios.s5.desc: The most ambitious scenario: a slash command starts the entire pipeline. Memory brings project context. One agent creates the plan. Another implements it. Hooks validate each write. A reviewer agent does code review. If approved, commits with a message based on the plan.
sec.receitas.intro: Ready-to-copy configurations you can adapt. Each recipe combines at least 3 different tools.
sec.receitas.r1.label: Recipe
sec.receitas.r1.title: Auto-Correction Hook + Pattern Memory
sec.receitas.r1.desc: The hook detects lint errors after each Write/Edit. Claude reads the error and fixes it. If the same type of error appears repeatedly in the session, it saves a feedback memory to prevent the pattern in future sessions.
sec.receitas.r2.label: Recipe
sec.receitas.r2.title: Orchestrator Agent that reuses Skills
sec.receitas.r2.desc: A custom agent uses the Skill tool internally to invoke existing skills like /commit or /review-pr. The agent decides which skill to call based on context, without you having to orchestrate manually.
sec.receitas.r3.label: Recipe
sec.receitas.r3.title: MCP + Plan + Memory for Sprint Planning
sec.receitas.r3.desc: A command queries the task tracker via MCP, cross-references with the codebase state, generates a work plan, and saves the sprint context to memory so all subsequent sessions are aware of what's in progress.
sec.receitas.r4.label: Recipe
sec.receitas.r4.title: CLAUDE.md that evolves via Hook + Memory
sec.receitas.r4.desc: A hook on the Stop event analyzes session feedback. When recurring patterns are detected, it suggests updates to CLAUDE.md. This way project rules evolve organically based on real usage — but always with human approval.
sec.metricas.intro: Tool integration directly impacts work quality and consistency. See the comparison between using isolated tools versus the integrated system.
sec.metricas.card.1.title: Rework
sec.metricas.card.2.title: Cross-session context
sec.metricas.card.3.title: Validation automation
sec.metricas.insight.title: The compounding effect
sec.metricas.insight.desc: Each integrated tool reduces friction for the next one. Memory reduces onboarding for each session. Hooks eliminate manual checks. Plans ensure the agent stays on scope. The gain isn't linear — it compounds. 3 integrated tools are worth more than 5 isolated ones.
sec.limites.intro: Integration adds power, but also complexity. Not every combination makes sense. Know when to simplify.
sec.limites.anti.1.title: Integration for its own sake
sec.limites.anti.1.desc: Connecting 6 tools for a task a single agent solves in 30 seconds. The coordination overhead exceeds any gains. If a single agent solves it, don't scale.
sec.limites.anti.2.title: Integrate when the problem demands it
sec.limites.anti.2.desc: Integration should arise from a real need: recurring errors justify hooks, loss of context across sessions justifies memory, complex tasks justify plan + agents. Start simple, add pieces as friction appears.
sec.limites.anti.3.title: Memory as a database
sec.limites.anti.3.desc: Saving everything to memory: logs, agent outputs, full conversation history. Memory has an index line limit. Store only what Claude needs in the next session and can't derive from the code or git.
sec.limites.anti.4.title: Selective and up-to-date Memory
sec.limites.anti.4.desc: Save decisions, preferences, and context that isn't in the code. Update or remove stale memories. One accurate, lean memory is worth more than hundreds of outdated entries.
sec.limites.anti.5.title: Hooks that do everything automatically
sec.limites.anti.5.desc: A hook that detects, fixes, commits, and pushes without human intervention. If something goes wrong, the damage is done before you notice. Hooks should validate and report, not take irreversible actions on their own.
sec.limites.anti.6.title: Hooks validate, humans approve
sec.limites.anti.6.desc: The hook checks and reports. Claude proposes the fix. You accept or reject. Irreversible actions (push, deploy, delete) always go through the human. The system suggests, but doesn't decide alone.
sec.limites.warning.title: The 3-minute rule
sec.limites.warning.desc: If you can't explain how the tools connect in your setup within 3 minutes, you probably have unnecessary complexity. An integrated setup should be understandable. If even you don't understand the flow, how will you debug when something breaks?
sec.proximos.intro: Start with one integration that solves a real problem. Then expand gradually.
sec.proximos.step.1.title: Set up a lint hook + auto-correction
sec.proximos.step.1.desc: Start with the simplest integration: a PostToolUse hook that runs ESLint or Ruff after each Write/Edit. Claude reads the errors and fixes them automatically.
sec.proximos.step.2.title: Create a command that uses Plan + Agent
sec.proximos.step.2.desc: Pick a recurring task (e.g., feature scaffold) and create a slash command that first generates a plan, then implements with a fresh agent.
sec.proximos.step.3.title: Set up project and feedback memories
sec.proximos.step.3.desc: Start saving architectural decisions and feedback about what works and what doesn't. Within a few sessions, Claude will have an automatic "onboarding" for your project.
sec.proximos.step.4.title: Build your first integrated pipeline
sec.proximos.step.4.desc: Combine everything: a command that reads memory, generates a plan, implements with an agent, validates with a hook, and reviews with another agent. Start with 3 tools and add more as needed.
sec.proximos.mantra.label: Part 8 Mantra:
sec.proximos.mantra.text: "Isolated tools are useful. Integrated tools are a system. The difference between using AI and having an AI workflow is in the connections you build between them — always with you as the architect who decides what to connect and when."
